title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Lucy Benton, who directs a children's theatre group.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're talking to Lucy Benton, who directs a children's theatre group. Lucy, you work with children who perform plays. How did this begin for you? || Guest: I did some acting at college, but I was never planning to be on stage full time. The real start came when [20]a teacher asked me to help shy children speak more clearly on stage[/20]. I thought it would be a one-week job, but I loved watching them grow braver. After that, I began running Saturday sessions at the community centre, and the group slowly became popular. || Interviewer: What was difficult when you were starting out? || Guest: Choosing the right play was the first big problem. I could find exciting stories, and the children were full of energy, but many scripts were not suitable. [21]The hardest part was choosing stories that were funny but still easy to understand[/21]. If the language was too difficult, the children worried about remembering it. If it was too simple, they got bored. I had to learn to change stories carefully. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy most about it now? || Guest: At the beginning of every session, I avoid giving them scripts straight away. [22]I always begin with movement games, because they make the children forget they are nervous[/22]. They jump, copy animals or act out weather, and soon they are laughing together. After that, speaking lines feels less frightening. It also helps new children join the group without feeling everyone is watching them. || Interviewer: Have you changed the way you work since then? || Guest: My biggest mistake was thinking children could work like adults if they were serious enough. For one show, [23]I once used a script that was much too long for the group[/23]. They tried hard, but rehearsals became tiring and the ending was rushed. Now I cut each play before we start and make sure every child has a part they can manage well. || Interviewer: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work? || Guest: Many parents expect acting to improve speaking, and it does. But they often notice something else first. [24]Parents are surprised by how much teamwork the children learn[/24]. They have to listen, help with costumes, move chairs and wait for their turn. A quiet child may become excellent at supporting others, even if they do not want the biggest role. || Interviewer: And what are your plans for the future? || Guest: Next year I would like the group to perform outside our usual hall. [25]I hope to take a small show into local libraries next summer[/25]. Libraries are friendly places, and families who cannot come to the theatre could still watch. We would use simple costumes and music, so the children could perform in a small space without needing lights or scenery. || Interviewer: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar? || Guest: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like children's theatre group, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal. || Interviewer: Thank you, that was really interesting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're talking to Lucy Benton, who directs a children's theatre group. Lucy, you work with children who perform plays. How did this begin for you?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I did some acting at college, but I was never planning to be on stage full time. The real start came when a teacher asked me to help shy children speak more clearly on stage. I thought it would be a one-week job, but I loved watching them grow braver. After that, I began running Saturday sessions at the community centre, and the group slowly became popular.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was difficult when you were starting out?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Choosing the right play was the first big problem. I could find exciting stories, and the children were full of energy, but many scripts were not suitable. The hardest part was choosing stories that were funny but still easy to understand. If the language was too difficult, the children worried about remembering it. If it was too simple, they got bored. I had to learn to change stories carefully.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy most about it now?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: At the beginning of every session, I avoid giving them scripts straight away. I always begin with movement games, because they make the children forget they are nervous. They jump, copy animals or act out weather, and soon they are laughing together. After that, speaking lines feels less frightening. It also helps new children join the group without feeling everyone is watching them.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Have you changed the way you work since then?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: My biggest mistake was thinking children could work like adults if they were serious enough. For one show, I once used a script that was much too long for the group. They tried hard, but rehearsals became tiring and the ending was rushed. Now I cut each play before we start and make sure every child has a part they can manage well.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Many parents expect acting to improve speaking, and it does. But they often notice something else first. Parents are surprised by how much teamwork the children learn. They have to listen, help with costumes, move chairs and wait for their turn. A quiet child may become excellent at supporting others, even if they do not want the biggest role.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: And what are your plans for the future?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Next year I would like the group to perform outside our usual hall. I hope to take a small show into local libraries next summer. Libraries are friendly places, and families who cannot come to the theatre could still watch. We would use simple costumes and music, so the children could perform in a small space without needing lights or scenery.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like children's theatre group, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Thank you, that was really interesting.

questions.question: Why did Lucy first start working with children in theatre?

options.B: A teacher invited her to help pupils.
options.C: She needed a job at a community centre.

questions.explanation: Lucy says a teacher asked her to help shy children speak more clearly on stage.

questions.question: What was difficult about choosing plays?

options.B: There were not enough exciting stories.
options.C: The stories had to be simple but interesting.

questions.explanation: Lucy says the hardest part was choosing stories that were funny but easy to understand.

questions.question: Why does Lucy use movement games?

options.A: They help children feel less nervous.
options.B: They teach children to dance well.
options.C: They replace the need for scripts.

questions.explanation: Lucy says movement games make children forget they are nervous.

questions.question: What mistake did Lucy make with one show?

options.A: She chose costumes that were too expensive.
options.B: She gave the children too much to learn.
options.C: She allowed too many children to join.

questions.explanation: Lucy used a script that was much too long for the group.

questions.question: What often surprises parents?

options.A: how loudly their children can speak
options.B: how quickly the children learn music
options.C: how much the children learn to work together

questions.explanation: Lucy says parents are surprised by how much teamwork the children learn.

questions.question: What does Lucy plan to do next summer?
questions.explanation: Lucy hopes to take a small show into local libraries next summer.
